INTRODUCTION\nThe Apollo 11 mission was the 11th in a series of flights using Apollo flight hard-\nware and was th', {'chunk_index': 27, 'chunk_start_position': 10800, 'chunk_end_position': 11300, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('\nThe Apollo 11 mission was the 11th in a series of flights using Apollo flight hard-\nware and was the first lunar landing mission of the Apollo Program. It was also the fifth\nmanned flight of the command and service modules and the third manned flight of the lunar\nmodule. The purpose of the mission was to perform a manned lunar landing and to return\nthe men safely to earth. The telescope is\nmechanically linked to the sextant so that it is operated when the sextant is operated.\nTo avoid driving the optics with a gimbal drive signal, or vice versa, the computer issues\ndiscrete signals which enable or disable the appropriate output. The drift is caused by an antibacklash spring.\nA regi', {'chunk_index': 473, 'chunk_start_position': 189200, 'chunk_end_position': 189700, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('ht testing) to drift\ntoward the positive stop. The drift is caused by an antibacklash spring.\nA register in the computer tracks trunnion position but is not large enough to pro-\nvide an unambiguous value for the full range of allowable trunnion angles. Therefore,\nthe register is biased to provide unambiguous readouts for the normally used range of\n-10° to +64.7°. In this case, the trunnion drifted beyond 64.7°, the register overflowed,\nand the computer lost track of actual trunnion position. MISSION SUPPORT PERFORMANCE\nFlight Control\nPreflight simulations provided adequate flight control training for all mission\nphases. Also, the flight controllers on the descent team supplemented this training by\nconducting descent simulations with the Apollo 12 crew. Interfaces between Mission\nControl teammembers and the flightcrew were effective, and no major ', {'chunk_index': 923, 'chunk_start_position': 369200, 'chunk_end_position': 369700, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('rew. Interfaces between Mission\nControl teammembers and the flightcrew were effective, and no major operational problems\nwere encountered. The two-way flow of information between the flightcrew and the flight\ncontrollers was effective. The overloading of the lunar module guidance computer during\npowered descent was assessed accurately, and the information provided to the flightcrew\npermitted continuation of descent.\nThe flight control response to those problems identified during the mission was\n', {'chunk_index': 924, 'chunk_start_position': 369600, 'chunk_end_position': 370100, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('nuation of descent.\nThe flight control response to those problems identified during the mission was\nbased on real-time data. Sections 8, 9, and 16 should be consulted for the postflight\nanalyses of these problems. Three of the more pertinent real-time decisions are dis-\ncussed in the following paragraphs.\nAt acquisition of signal after lunar orbit insertion, data showed that the indicated\ntank B nitrogen pressure was approximately 300 psi lower than expected and that the\npressure had started to ', {'chunk_index': 925, 'chunk_start_position': 370000, 'chunk_end_position': 370500, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('itrogen pressure was approximately 300 psi lower than expected and that the\npressure had started to decrease at 80 seconds into the maneuver. ASSESSMENT OF MISSION OBJECTIVES\nThe single primary mission objective for the Apollo 11 mission (as defined in the\nNASA Headquarters document OMSF M-D MA 500-11 (SE 010-000-1) entitled "Apollo Flight\nMission Assignments" and prepared July 11, 1969) was to perform a man', {'chunk_index': 951, 'chunk_start_position': 380400, 'chunk_end_position': 380900, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('000-1) entitled "Apollo Flight\nMission Assignments" and prepared July 11, 1969) was to perform a manned lunar land-\ning and return safely to earth. In addition to the single primary objective, 11 sec-\nondary objectives were delineated from the following two general categories:\n1. To perform selenological inspection and sampling\n2. To obtain data to assess the capability and limitations of a man and his\nequipment in the lunar environment\nThe 11 secondary objectives are listed in table 14-I and ar', {'chunk_index': 952, 'chunk_start_position': 380800, 'chunk_end_position': 381300, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('d his\nequipment in the lunar environment\nThe 11 secondary objectives are listed in table 14-I and are described in detail in\nNASA Manned Spacecraft Center document SPD 9-R-038 (entitled "Mission Requirements,\nSA-506/CSM-107/LM-5, G Type Mission Lunar Landing," April 17, 1969).\nThe following experiments were assigned to the Apollo 11 mission:\n1. Passive seismic experiment (S-031)\n2. Lunar field geology (S-059)\n3. Laser ranging retroreflector (S-078)\n4. Solar wind composition (S-080)\n5. Cosmic ray', {'chunk_index': 953, 'chunk_start_position': 381200, 'chunk_end_position': 381700, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('logy (S-059)\n3. Laser ranging retroreflector (S-078)\n4. Solar wind composition (S-080)\n5. Cosmic ray detection (S-151)\nThe single primary objective was met. LAUNCH VEHICLE SUMMARY\nThe trajectory parameters of the AS-506 launch vehicle from launch to translunar\ninjection were all close to the expected values. The vehicle was launched on an\nazimuth 90° east of north. A roll maneuver was initiated at 13.2 seconds in order to\nplace the vehicle on the planned flight azimuth of 72.058° east of north.\nFollowing lunar module ejection, the S-IVB/instrument unit maneuvered to a sling-\nshot attitude that was fixed relative to the local horizon', {'chunk_index': 965, 'chunk_start_position': 386000, 'chunk_end_position': 386500, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('VB/instrument unit maneuvered to a sling-\nshot attitude that was fixed relative to the local horizontal. The retrograde velocity\nnecessary to perform the lunar slingshot maneuver was accomplished by a liquid oxygen\ndump, an auxiliary propulsion system firing, and liquid hydrogen venting. The closest\napproach of the vehicle to the lunar surface was 1825 miles at 78:42:00. CONCLUSIONS\nThe Apollo 11 mission, which included a manned lunar landing and lunar surface ex-\nploration, was conducted with skill', {'chunk_index': 1121, 'chunk_start_position': 448400, 'chunk_end_position': 448900, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('ion, which included a manned lunar landing and lunar surface ex-\nploration, was conducted with skill, precision, and relative ease. The excellent per-\nformance of the spacecraft in the preceding four flights and the thorough planning in all\naspects of the program permitted the safe and efficient execution of this mission. The\nfollowing conclusions are drawn from the information contained in this report.\n1. The effectiveness of preflight training was reflected in the skill and precision\nwith whic', {'chunk_index': 1122, 'chunk_start_position': 448800, 'chunk_end_position': 449300, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ('eport.\n1. The effectiveness of preflight training was reflected in the skill and precision\nwith which the crew executed the lunar landing. Manual control while maneuvering to the\ndesired landing point was satisfactorily exercised.\n2. The planned techniques involved in the guidance, navigation, and control of the\ndescent trajectory were good. Performance of the landing radar met all expectations in\nproviding the information required for descent.\n3. The extravehicular mobility units were adequatel', {'chunk_index': 1123, 'chunk_start_position': 449200, 'chunk_end_position': 449700, 'chunk_source': 'NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text', 'chunk_mission': 'apollo_11', 'document_category': 'nasa_archive', 'source_file_path': 'data_text/apollo11/NASA_NTRS_Archive_19710015566_textract_full_text.txt'}), ("\nproviding the information required for descent.\n3. The extravehicular mobility units were adequately designed to enable the crew\nto conduct the planned activities. Adaptation to the 1/6-g environment was relatively\nquick, and mobility on the lunar surface was easy.\n4. The two-man prelaunch checkout and countdown for ascent from the lunar surface\nwere well planned and executed.\n5.
